food_subgroup: Beef — grilled or pan-seared steak cuts

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM represents grilled beef meat; used as closest parent as no specific Turkish beef steak code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of beef steak is approximately 200 g, representing one grilled steak portion as commonly served at Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #23527
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled beef top loin steak (lean only, cooked). This is the closest defensible match for a Turkish-style grilled beef steak served at restaurant level. Turkish beef steak preparation (biftek) uses similar lean beef cuts grilled with minimal seasoning, making this USDA entry the most appropriate approved-source anchor. Sodium adjusted upward to 75 mg per 100 g to reflect typical restaurant seasoning with salt.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of pure protein and fat foods — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consensus
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure grilled beef steak contains negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently assign a GI of 0 to pure meat products as they contain no glycemic carbohydrates. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for non-glycemic foods, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) to satisfy numeric validation requirements; gi_category is low; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. This is consistent with established clinical nutrition practice for protein-dominant, carbohydrate-free foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of beef steak contributes approximately 150 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request minimal added salt and avoid high-sodium sauces or marinades commonly served alongside.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled beef steak contains virtually no carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of effectively zero, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 6.8 g saturated fat and 172 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose lean cuts, and prefer grilling over frying to minimise additional fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef steak is a thick cut of beef — typically from the sirloin, ribeye, tenderloin, or striploin — that is grilled or pan-seared to varying degrees of doneness. In the Turkish culinary tradition, beef steak (known as biftek) is seasoned simply with salt, black pepper, olive oil, and sometimes garlic or fresh herbs such as thyme or rosemary, then cooked over high heat to develop a caramelised crust while retaining a juicy interior. At Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore, beef steak is served as a main course, typically accompanied by grilled vegetables, rice pilaf, or flatbread. Nutritionally, beef steak is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 207 kcal, 30.5 g protein, and 9 g fat per 100 g. It is an excellent source of haem iron, zinc, vitamin B12, niacin, and selenium — nutrients critical for red blood cell production, immune function, and neurological health. The negligible carbohydrate content means it has an effectively zero glycemic index, making it appropriate for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. Saturated fat content (approximately 3.4 g per 100 g) warrants moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and when prepared with halal-certified beef, it is suitable for Muslim consumers. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: Is beef steak good for diabetics?
How many calories in a beef steak?
Is beef steak high in protein?
How much saturated fat is in beef steak?
Can I eat beef steak if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in beef steak?

confidence_reason: No Singapore-specific or Turkish-specific approved nutrient database entry exists for restaurant-style beef steak. Values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for grilled beef top loin steak (lean only), which is the closest defensible approved-source match. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant seasoning. Confidence is medium rather than high due to this adaptation.
notes: carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — grilled lean beef contains no glycemic carbohydrates; this is naturally zero for a pure meat product. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero for plain grilled beef with no added sugars. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as beef contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked beef. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for lean grilled beef. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for carbohydrate-free protein foods. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #174032. No -1 sentinel values remain in this record as all fields were obtainable from the USDA source. Halal status is assumed based on restaurant context (Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine); formal halal certification not independently verified. Turkish cuisine does not require additional mandatory local-language names beyond the Singaporean multilingual set applied here, as the dish is served in Singapore. The food_name has been normalised from '34. Beef Steak' (menu-style with numbering) to canonical 'Beef Steak'.
